writeup.title: Film thickness (250 nm vs. 470 nm) shows no consistent or meaningful effect on Tc in Ta-Hf (83:17) thin films; annealing temperature is the dominant process variable.
writeup.hypothesis: In Ta-Hf (83:17) thin films, increasing film thickness is associated with a systematic change (decrease or increase) in superconducting critical temperature Tc.
writeup.summary: Across 8 Yang_2026 Ta-Hf (83:17) samples spanning only two discrete thicknesses (250 nm and 470 nm), the Tc differences attributable to thickness are at most 0.16 K, inconsistent in direction across annealing conditions, and smaller than the within-group Tc variation driven by annealing temperature alone (up to 0.37 K). No reliable thickness-Tc relationship can be established from this dataset. The negative result is itself physically informative: at these thicknesses, both films are well above the length scales where thickness suppression of Tc is expected to be significant, and thermal processing dominates Tc.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: The largest thickness-associated Tc difference occurs at 850°C annealing, where the 470 nm film yields Tc = 6.00 K versus 5.84 K for the 250 nm film — a 0.16 K increase with thickness that provides the clearest directional signal in the dataset. A similar but smaller positive trend (0.04 K) is observed at 750°C.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: At 650°C annealing, the direction reverses: the 250 nm film has a marginally higher Tc (6.20 K) than the 470 nm film (6.18 K), and at 550°C the difference is only 0.04 K — both within plausible measurement uncertainty. The inconsistency of direction across annealing conditions undermines any claim of a systematic thickness effect.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Samples spanning a continuous, broad range of thicknesses (e.g., 10–600 nm) at fixed annealing conditions are needed to isolate thickness as a variable; the current two-point, thick-film-only dataset cannot resolve a thickness-Tc relationship.

writeup.finding_detail: The 8 Yang_2026 Ta-Hf (83:17) samples are evenly split between 250 nm (samples 1–4, annealed at 550–850°C) and 470 nm (samples 5–8, same annealing series). Within each thickness group, Tc varies by up to 0.37 K as a function of annealing temperature, demonstrating that thermal treatment is the primary process variable. Pairwise comparison at matched annealing temperatures yields thickness-associated Tc differences of +0.04 K (550°C), −0.02 K (650°C), +0.04 K (750°C), and +0.16 K (850°C). The sign reversal at 650°C and the near-zero differences at 550°C and 750°C are inconsistent with a monotonic thickness-Tc relationship. The largest difference (0.16 K at 850°C) may reflect differential microstructural evolution under high thermal budget rather than a direct thickness effect, as thicker films may retain distinct α/β-Ta-Hf phase character under identical annealing conditions. The seeding correlation from Chang_2025 describes proximity-effect suppression of the superconducting gap by an Au overlayer — a qualitatively different mechanism that does not predict intrinsic Tc changes in bare alloy films and should not be interpreted as supporting this hypothesis.
writeup.alternative_explanations: Annealing temperature is the dominant determinant of Tc in this system, likely through control of crystal phase composition (α vs. β-Ta-Hf) and grain structure; any apparent thickness effect at high annealing temperatures may reflect thickness-dependent microstructural response to thermal budget rather than an intrinsic thickness-Tc relationship.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is very low (0.2) due to: (1) only two discrete thickness values, both in the thick-film regime (250 nm and 470 nm) where thickness effects on Tc are physically expected to be negligible; (2) only 8 total samples from a single study and single material composition; (3) observed thickness-associated Tc differences (0.00–0.16 K) are at or below typical transport measurement uncertainty (±0.05–0.1 K) for most conditions; (4) inconsistent directionality across annealing conditions; and (5) the seeding author-stated correlation derives from a mechanistically distinct physical context (Au proximity effect) that does not apply here.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM mapping function of the form Tc = f(film_thickness) is not supportable for Ta-Hf (83:17) at this stage. The data do not justify including film thickness as an independent predictor of Tc in any materials-to-device mapping layer for this alloy system at thicknesses ≥250 nm. If a thickness term were to be included in a QREM model, it would need to be conditioned on annealing temperature and validated against a much broader thickness range (particularly sub-50 nm films where surface/interface effects become significant). The current evidence suggests that annealing temperature should be the primary process variable encoded in any QREM mapping for Ta-Hf Tc prediction, not film thickness.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: Re-analysis focused on thickness-Tc relationships is not warranted for Ta-Hf (83:17) with the current dataset. No other material classes in the corpus are represented in this hypothesis. If future data collection includes Ta-Hf films below 100 nm or spans a continuous thickness range, a focused re-analysis would be appropriate. For comparison, pure Ta or Nb datasets with controlled thickness variation (if available in the corpus) would be more informative for establishing whether a thickness-Tc mapping function is physically meaningful in this material family.
questions_for_reviewer: Is the 0.16 K Tc difference observed at 850°C annealing (250 nm vs. 470 nm) within the expected measurement uncertainty for transport-based Tc determination in this experimental setup, or is it likely a real physical difference? This determines whether even the strongest signal in the dataset is meaningful.

Is there a known mechanism by which film thickness in the 250–470 nm range would differentially affect α/β-Ta-Hf phase stability under high-temperature annealing (≥750°C), which could explain the apparent thickness effect at high annealing temperatures without invoking a direct thickness-Tc relationship?

The seeding correlation is attributed to Chang_2025 and describes an Au-encapsulation proximity effect. Was this correlation included in the seed set in error (wrong mechanism), or is there a separate rationale for expecting intrinsic thickness-Tc dependence in bare Ta-Hf films that should be documented?

At what film thickness would you expect quantum size effects or surface/interface suppression of superconductivity to become significant for Ta-Hf alloys — is 250 nm already safely in the bulk-like regime, or could there be residual thin-film effects worth probing?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next experiment would be a systematic deposition series of Ta-Hf (83:17) films spanning 10–500 nm thickness at a single fixed annealing condition (e.g., 650°C, where the current data show the least annealing-driven variation), with Tc measured by transport and phase composition confirmed by XRD. This would isolate thickness as the sole variable and determine whether any thickness-Tc relationship exists in the physically relevant thin-film regime (<100 nm) for quantum device applications.

writeup.title: No monotonic relationship between internal quality factor and sheet resistance is supported in NbSe2 microwave resonators across six early-exploration samples.
writeup.hypothesis: Higher sheet resistance in superconducting NbSe2 films correlates monotonically with lower internal quality factor (Qi), such that sheet resistance can serve as a predictor of microwave loss.
writeup.summary: Analysis of six NbSe2 resonator samples from a single study (Zaman_2025) reveals no consistent monotonic relationship between sheet resistance and Qi_internal. The highest Qi (60,000) occurs at an intermediate sheet resistance (60 Ω/sq), while both the lowest-resistance clean-limit sample (10 Ω/sq, Qi = 7,000) and the highest-resistance sample (240 Ω/sq, Qi = 8,000) exhibit low Qi, and near-identical sheet resistances (25 vs. 29 Ω/sq) yield Qi values differing by more than threefold. This negative result is itself informative: sheet resistance alone appears to be an unreliable predictor of microwave loss in these NbSe2 devices, with uncontrolled confounders — most plausibly surface/interface quality, flake uniformity, or two-level system (TLS) density — likely dominating Qi variability.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: Sample Zaman_2025_D1 (240 Ω/sq, Qi = 8,000) is loosely consistent with a narrative in which extreme disorder degrades coherence, and the single author-stated correlation provides weak prior motivation for the hypothesis. However, neither observation constitutes systematic support for a monotonic trend.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Zaman_2025_D8 (10 Ω/sq, clean-limit) has one of the lowest Qi values (7,000), directly contradicting the prediction that lower sheet resistance yields higher Qi; additionally, Zaman_2025_D3 (29 Ω/sq, Qi = 7,000) and Zaman_2025_D4 (25 Ω/sq, Qi = 24,000) show >3× Qi variation at nearly identical sheet resistances, indicating that uncontrolled variables dominate the response.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: A controlled NbSe2 film series in which sheet resistance is varied by a single parameter (e.g., flake thickness or deposition conditions) while all other fabrication and measurement variables are held constant, accompanied by independent TLS loss tangent measurements and microstructural characterization (XRD, Raman, TEM), would be the most decisive experiment to resolve whether any true Qi–Rs relationship exists.

writeup.finding_detail: Across the six available NbSe2 samples, the Qi–sheet resistance relationship is non-monotonic and inconsistent. The peak Qi of 60,000 in Zaman_2025_D2 (60 Ω/sq, dirty_limit) is isolated and could reflect a non-monotonic optimum, but it is a single data point and may instead reflect superior flake quality or interface cleanliness unrelated to sheet resistance. The clean-limit sample Zaman_2025_D8 (10 Ω/sq) achieves only Qi = 7,000, which is the strongest single counterexample to the hypothesis: if lower sheet resistance indicated less disorder and fewer quasiparticle losses, this sample should outperform all others. The near-identical sheet resistances of D3 (29 Ω/sq) and D4 (25 Ω/sq) yielding Qi of 7,000 and 24,000 respectively demonstrate that the Qi variance within a narrow Rs window exceeds any trend across the full Rs range, strongly implicating uncontrolled confounders. The sole literature seed (Böttcher_2025) concerns NbN CPW resonators processed by atomic layer etching — a different material, geometry, and fabrication route — and explicitly reports no correlation between normal-state resistance and Qi, providing weak and arguably contradictory prior support rather than positive evidence. With n = 6 samples all from a single study and all classified as early-exploration coherence tier, no statistically meaningful inference is possible, and the scatter is fully consistent with noise or hidden variables rather than a true Qi–Rs relationship.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that TLS losses at the NbSe2 surface, substrate interface, or native oxide dominate Qi variability and are controlled primarily by flake uniformity, surface cleanliness, and exfoliation quality — all of which are largely independent of sheet resistance but co-vary with it through the shared underlying variable of microstructural disorder. A secondary alternative is that kinetic inductance fraction (which scales with sheet resistance) redistributes microwave current density and alters lossy-interface participation ratios in a non-monotonic fashion, precluding a simple linear mapping.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set near the floor of the scale (0.15) because: (1) only 6 samples exist, all from a single study and single material, making statistical inference meaningless; (2) the data show no consistent directional trend — the hypothesis is contradicted by multiple specific samples rather than merely unsupported by absence of evidence; (3) the one author-stated correlation seed derives from a different material (NbN) and reports no correlation; (4) all samples are classified as early-exploration coherence tier, indicating high measurement uncertainty; and (5) the scatter between near-identical sheet resistance values (D3 vs. D4) is larger than any apparent trend across the full Rs range, consistent with dominant uncontrolled confounders. A small residual confidence above zero is retained because the dataset is too sparse to fully exclude a weak or non-monotonic relationship that a larger, better-controlled dataset might reveal.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM materials-to-device mapping function of the form Qi = f(sheet_resistance) is not supportable for NbSe2 at this time. The data do not justify even a monotonic mapping (e.g., Qi ∝ Rs^α for any sign of α), let alone a quantitative one. If a mapping were to be attempted, the non-monotonic scatter suggests it would require at minimum a two-parameter model incorporating both sheet resistance and a proxy for surface/interface quality (e.g., RMS roughness, oxide thickness, or TLS density), but no such model can be parameterized from the current six samples. For QREM purposes, sheet resistance should not be used as a standalone predictor of Qi for NbSe2 resonators in the early-exploration regime; it may at best serve as one input in a multi-variable model once a larger, controlled dataset is available. The finding is consistent with the broader QREM principle that transport properties and microwave loss properties share microstructural origins but are not directly causally linked.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: This hypothesis should not be re-run as currently formulated until a substantially larger dataset is available. NbSe2 is the only material with paired Qi and sheet resistance measurements in the corpus (n = 6), and this sample count is insufficient for meaningful per-material analysis. No other material in the current corpus has enough samples to warrant a focused re-analysis of this specific hypothesis. If NbSe2 data expand to n ≥ 20 samples with controlled fabrication provenance, a re-analysis would be warranted. For cross-material comparison, NbN and TiN are the most natural candidates given their established use in kinetic inductance detectors and qubits, but they currently lack paired measurements in this corpus.
questions_for_reviewer: Zaman_2025_D2 (60 Ω/sq, dirty_limit, Qi = 60,000) is a strong outlier relative to all other samples. Is there a known fabrication or measurement distinction for this sample — such as a different substrate, post-processing step, or resonator geometry — that could explain its anomalously high Qi independently of sheet resistance?

The clean-limit sample D8 (10 Ω/sq) has one of the lowest Qi values despite presumably the least disorder. Is this consistent with known NbSe2 physics, where the clean limit may introduce different loss channels (e.g., nodal quasiparticles from the charge density wave gap structure) that are absent or suppressed in the dirty limit?

The transport regime labels (clean_limit, dirty_limit, intermediate) appear to encode microstructural information beyond sheet resistance alone. Do these labels correspond to independently measured mean free path or disorder parameters, or are they inferred from sheet resistance thresholds? If the latter, they may not add independent information.

Given that NbSe2 is a van der Waals material with a charge density wave transition near 33 K, how sensitive are the reported Qi values to the measurement temperature relative to both Tc (~7 K) and T_CDW (~33 K)? Could CDW domain fluctuations be a dominant and sheet-resistance-independent loss mechanism at the measurement temperatures used?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next experiment would be a controlled NbSe2 film series in which a single fabrication parameter (e.g., number of exfoliated layers or sputtering thickness for thin-film NbSe2) is systematically varied to span sheet resistances from ~5 to ~300 Ω/sq, while substrate preparation, resonator geometry, and measurement protocol are held strictly constant. Each sample should be accompanied by: (1) Raman spectroscopy and XRD to quantify crystalline quality and CDW order independently of Rs; (2) temperature-dependent Qi measurements to separate quasiparticle and TLS loss contributions; and (3) surface oxide characterization by XPS. A minimum of 15–20 samples would be needed to permit meaningful statistical analysis of the Qi–Rs relationship while controlling for these confounders.

writeup.title: Film thickness positively correlates with Tc in NbSe2 thin films, but transport regime co-variation prevents clean causal attribution to thickness alone
writeup.hypothesis: In NbSe2 thin films, increasing film thickness is positively correlated with higher superconducting critical temperature (Tc), reflecting bulk-like superconductivity emerging as 2D quantum confinement is reduced.
writeup.summary: Across 6 NbSe2 samples from a single study (Zaman_2025), Tc rises monotonically from 3.5 K at 1.99 nm to 7.0 K at 7.98–10.07 nm, consistent with the well-known suppression of superconductivity in ultrathin 2D films and convergence toward the bulk NbSe2 Tc of ~7.2 K. However, the transport regime classification (dirty_limit → intermediate → clean_limit) co-varies systematically with thickness across the same sample set, making it impossible from this dataset alone to determine whether Tc is controlled by thickness per se or by film quality and disorder level, which are themselves correlated with thickness. The finding is assessed as partially supported but flagged as a derived-field artifact concern due to this confound.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: All 6 samples follow a strict monotonic positive trend (Tc: 3.5 → 4.8 → 5.5 → 6.0 → 7.0 → 7.0 K; thickness: 1.99 → 4.08 → 5.86 → 7.13 → 7.98 → 10.07 nm), with saturation near the bulk Tc at the thicker end — a physically expected and internally self-consistent pattern. The thinnest sample (D1, 1.99 nm) shows the lowest Tc and is classified as dirty_limit, while the thickest (D8, 10.07 nm) reaches 7.0 K and is classified as clean_limit.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Samples D5 (7.98 nm) and D8 (10.07 nm) share identical Tc values of 7.0 K despite a ~2 nm thickness difference, indicating saturation and non-linearity that limits predictive modeling. More critically, the transport regime variable co-varies with thickness across all samples, so the observed Tc trend may reflect improving film quality rather than a direct thickness-driven quantum confinement effect.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Independent replication from a second research group or deposition method is the most critical gap; without it, the entire trend rests on a single-source dataset and cannot be distinguished from preparation-condition artifacts. Structural characterization (XRD, STEM) co-measured with electrical data would also help decouple disorder from thickness as the controlling variable.

writeup.finding_detail: The 6 NbSe2 samples from Zaman_2025 span a thickness range of approximately 1.99 nm to 10.07 nm and display a clear, monotonic positive Tc trend with no outliers or reversals. The thinnest sample (D1, 1.99 nm, Tc=3.5 K) is classified as dirty_limit transport, while the thickest (D8, 10.07 nm, Tc=7.0 K) is classified as clean_limit, with intermediate samples (D2–D4) in a transitional regime. This co-variation between transport regime and thickness is the central confound: if film quality (disorder, surface oxidation, substrate coupling) systematically improves with thickness in this sample set, then the Tc trend may be a proxy for quality rather than a direct manifestation of reduced 2D quantum confinement. The saturation of Tc at 7.0 K for both D5 and D8 is physically consistent with convergence to the bulk NbSe2 value (~7.2 K), lending mechanistic plausibility to the thickness interpretation, but this alone cannot resolve the confound. The seeding correlation from Chang_2025 involves Au encapsulation proximity effects in a Ta film — a mechanistically distinct phenomenon in a different material — and provides no independent corroboration for the NbSe2 thickness-Tc relationship.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that reduced disorder scattering (rather than reduced 2D confinement per se) is the proximate driver of higher Tc, with film thickness acting as a proxy for overall film quality; thinner films may have higher surface-to-volume ratios, greater oxidation sensitivity, and stronger substrate-induced pair-breaking, all of which would produce the same Tc-thickness trend without requiring a quantum confinement mechanism. Substrate-induced strain or charge transfer that diminishes as film thickness increases is a secondary alternative that cannot be excluded without capping-layer or substrate-variation experiments.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at 0.65, above the threshold for a partially supported finding but below the 0.70 level that would warrant stronger claims. The monotonic trend across all 6 samples with no contradicting data points and physically plausible saturation behavior justifies exceeding 0.5. Confidence is capped at 0.65 due to: (1) single-source dependency — all 6 samples originate from Zaman_2025, with no independent replication; (2) small n=6 sample size; (3) the transport regime confound, which is the primary reason this finding is flagged as a derived-field artifact concern rather than a clean positive; and (4) the seeding correlation from Chang_2025 being mechanistically irrelevant to this system and therefore providing no corroborating weight.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM materials-to-device mapping function linking film_thickness_nm to Tc_K for NbSe2 is directionally supported but not yet reliably parameterizable from this dataset. The observed trend is consistent with a monotonically increasing, saturating function (e.g., a Tc(d) ~ Tc_bulk × [1 - exp(-d/d0)] form, or a power-law approach to bulk Tc), with approximate parameters Tc_bulk ≈ 7.0–7.2 K and a characteristic length scale d0 in the 3–5 nm range suggested by the data. However, implementing such a mapping function in QREM would be premature without: (a) independent replication to confirm the functional form is not preparation-specific, (b) resolution of the disorder-versus-confinement confound (which would determine whether the mapping input should be thickness, mean free path, or a combined quality metric), and (c) sub-2 nm data to anchor the low-thickness end. At present, the mapping layer should treat this as a qualitative directional prior — thicker NbSe2 films trend toward higher Tc — rather than a quantitative transfer function.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: NbSe2 is the only material with sufficient samples (n=6) in this corpus to warrant a focused re-analysis of the thickness-Tc relationship. No other material in the 193-sample corpus has been identified as having a comparable thickness series. A targeted re-analysis of NbSe2 should be conducted if additional samples from other sources can be ingested; the current single-study limitation is the binding constraint. Materials such as NbN and TaN, which have well-documented thickness-dependent Tc behavior in the literature, would be high-value targets for corpus expansion to test generalizability of the relationship across 2D superconductor classes.
questions_for_reviewer: Is the transport regime classification (dirty_limit, intermediate, clean_limit) in the Zaman_2025 dataset derived post-hoc from the measured Tc and resistivity values, or was it assigned independently from structural/compositional characterization? If derived from transport data, this variable cannot be treated as an independent confound and the causal ambiguity is even more severe.

In the NbSe2 thin-film literature, is there consensus on whether Tc suppression at sub-5 nm thickness is primarily attributable to quantum confinement (reduced Cooper pair coherence length in the z-direction) versus surface/interface disorder? Specifically, do encapsulated or hBN-capped NbSe2 films of similar thickness show the same Tc suppression, which would argue against oxidation/disorder as the dominant mechanism?

The saturation of Tc at 7.0 K for both D5 (7.98 nm) and D8 (10.07 nm) is below the commonly cited bulk NbSe2 Tc of ~7.2 K. Is this 0.2 K gap within measurement uncertainty for these samples, or does it suggest a persistent thin-film suppression even at 10 nm that a simple confinement model would not predict?

Are there known systematic differences in exfoliation or transfer yield as a function of NbSe2 flake thickness that could introduce a selection bias — e.g., thicker flakes being easier to transfer cleanly — which would confound the thickness-quality relationship in this dataset?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is ingestion of NbSe2 thickness-series data from at least one independent group using a different preparation method (e.g., MBE-grown or hBN-encapsulated exfoliated flakes), with co-measured structural quality metrics (RRR, XRD coherence length, or STEM imaging). This would simultaneously address the single-source limitation and the disorder-versus-confinement confound. If encapsulated samples show the same Tc-thickness trend as unencapsulated ones, the quantum confinement interpretation is substantially strengthened; if encapsulated samples show higher Tc at equivalent thickness, surface disorder is implicated as the dominant suppression mechanism.

writeup.title: No thickness-Tc correlation can be assessed for PtSi: all 6 corpus samples share identical film thickness (25 nm) and critical temperature (0.9 K), yielding zero variance in both variables.
writeup.hypothesis: Changes in PtSi film thickness produce measurable changes in superconducting critical temperature (Tc), such that a meaningful correlation between film_thickness_nm and Tc_K exists in this material system.
writeup.summary: All 6 PtSi samples drawn from Nanayakkara_2026 record identical values of Tc_K = 0.9 K and film_thickness_nm = 25.0 nm, making it statistically impossible to evaluate any thickness-Tc relationship. The dataset represents a single point in the (thickness, Tc) parameter space, not a distribution, so neither a positive nor a negative correlation can be inferred. This is an informative null result: the corpus as currently structured does not contain the data needed to test this hypothesis for PtSi.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: There is no supporting evidence for the hypothesis. The uniform Tc_K = 0.9 K across all 6 samples is consistent with a thickness-independent Tc at 25 nm, but this consistency is an artifact of the single-thickness dataset rather than evidence of any relationship.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: Zero variance in both film_thickness_nm and Tc_K across all 6 samples makes correlation analysis undefined; additionally, the numeric suffixes in sample names (e.g., ASQ-50-100, SSQ-25, ScS-100) suggest that the parameter varied across Nanayakkara_2026 device variants is junction or gap geometry, not film thickness, meaning the film_thickness_nm field may not capture the full dimensional parameter space of this study.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: PtSi samples spanning a systematic range of film thicknesses (e.g., 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 50 nm) deposited and measured under identical conditions are the minimum requirement to test this hypothesis; sub-10 nm samples are especially important for detecting disorder- or confinement-driven Tc suppression.

writeup.finding_detail: All six records — Nanayakkara_2026_ASQ-50-100, Nanayakkara_2026_SSQ-25, Nanayakkara_2026_SSQ-50, Nanayakkara_2026_ScS-100, Nanayakkara_2026_ScS-25, and Nanayakkara_2026_ScS-50 — report Tc_K = 0.9 K and film_thickness_nm = 25.0 nm without exception. The numeric suffixes in sample names (25, 50, 100) most plausibly encode a junction length or weak-link gap parameter rather than film thickness, yet this geometric variation is not reflected in the structured film_thickness_nm field, raising a derived-field concern about whether the corpus schema captures the full parameter space of the Nanayakkara_2026 study. The single seeding literature reference (Chang_2025, Ta/Au bilayer) describes an inverse proximity effect mechanism in a bilayer system that is physically distinct from bulk Tc suppression in a single-layer silicide, and provides no transferable quantitative evidence for PtSi. At 25 nm, PtSi may already be in a thickness regime where Tc is saturated at its bulk-like value, meaning the absence of Tc variation at this single thickness is not evidence against a thickness dependence at smaller scales. The corpus is therefore uninformative with respect to the hypothesis, and the 0.9 K value should be treated as a single material constant for PtSi at 25 nm, not as a data point in a thickness-dependent trend.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that all Nanayakkara_2026 samples were fabricated from the same PtSi film batch at a fixed 25 nm thickness, with device-to-device variation encoded in junction geometry (the name suffixes) rather than film thickness; in this case, the hypothesis is simply untestable from this source. A secondary alternative is that 25 nm PtSi lies above the thickness threshold for Tc suppression, so no variation would be expected even if a threshold exists at smaller thicknesses.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is set at the minimum meaningful level (0.1) because the corpus provides exactly zero degrees of freedom for correlation analysis: both variables are constant across all 6 samples. A confidence of 0.0 would imply the hypothesis is falsified, which is not the case — it is simply untestable from this data. The seeding reference (n=1 author-stated correlation) concerns a different material and mechanism and contributes no transferable statistical weight. No upward adjustment is warranted given the single-source, single-material, single-thickness nature of the dataset.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM materials-to-device mapping function of the form Tc = f(film_thickness) for PtSi cannot be constructed or parameterized from this corpus. The current data supports only a point estimate: Tc(PtSi, 25 nm) ≈ 0.9 K, with no uncertainty bounds on thickness dependence. Until multi-thickness PtSi data are available, any QREM layer that requires a thickness-dependent Tc model for PtSi must either (a) treat Tc as a fixed material constant at 0.9 K regardless of thickness, with an explicit flag that this assumption is unvalidated below ~25 nm, or (b) borrow a functional form (e.g., the Finkel'stein disorder-driven suppression model or a BKT-type thin-film model) from better-characterized silicide or transition-metal systems and apply it with very wide uncertainty bounds. Neither approach is well-supported by current evidence, and the QREM mapping layer should surface this gap explicitly to downstream users.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: This hypothesis should not be re-run for PtSi until additional multi-thickness samples are ingested into the corpus; re-running on the current 6-sample single-thickness dataset will reproduce this null result identically. For other material classes, a focused re-analysis is warranted only for materials with ≥4 samples spanning at least two distinct film thicknesses. Based on the current corpus scope described in this assessment, no other material meets that criterion within this hypothesis key; a corpus-wide audit of film_thickness_nm variance by material is recommended before scheduling re-analyses.
questions_for_reviewer: Do the numeric suffixes in Nanayakkara_2026 sample names (e.g., SSQ-25, SSQ-50, ScS-100) encode a junction gap length or weak-link length in nanometers, and if so, should a new hypothesis key be opened for Tc_K vs. junction_gap_nm in PtSi Josephson devices rather than Tc_K vs. film_thickness_nm?

Is 25 nm a physically meaningful lower bound for PtSi film thickness in the Nanayakkara_2026 fabrication process, or were thinner films attempted and excluded from the study — and if excluded, on what basis (e.g., film continuity, resistivity threshold)?

Does the Nanayakkara_2026 source report any sheet resistance or residual resistivity ratio (RRR) data that could serve as a disorder proxy, allowing an indirect test of whether disorder-driven Tc suppression is relevant at 25 nm in this PtSi system?

Should the film_thickness_nm schema field be split into 'superconducting_layer_thickness_nm' and 'normal_metal_layer_thickness_nm' to correctly represent bilayer and proximity-effect device geometries (as in Chang_2025) without conflating them with single-layer thickness in systems like PtSi?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is a targeted literature search and corpus ingestion for PtSi thin films with systematically varied thicknesses, specifically seeking studies that measure Tc (or the superconducting gap) for PtSi films in the 5–50 nm range on comparable substrates. If no such studies exist in the published literature, this gap should be flagged as a priority experimental need for the PtSi-based qubit community, given that thickness control is a standard fabrication variable and its effect on Tc in this silicide system appears uncharacterized in the accessible corpus.

writeup.title: No consistent thickness–Tc relationship is detectable in a 4-sample ALD-NbN dataset spanning 5–50 nm and 10–12 K
writeup.hypothesis: In NbN thin films, greater film thickness is associated with higher superconducting critical temperature (Tc), or conversely, thinner films exhibit suppressed Tc due to proximity effects, disorder, or surface/interface effects.
writeup.summary: Across the four available ALD-deposited NbN samples (film_thickness_nm = 5–50 nm; Tc_K = 10.0–12.0 K), no consistent monotonic relationship between film thickness and Tc is observed. The thickest film (50 nm, Wu_2026 on AlN-sapphire) shares the lowest Tc (10.0 K) with the thinnest film (5 nm, Wu_2026 on silicon), while a 20 nm film (Böttcher_2025) achieves the highest Tc (12.0 K), directly contradicting any simple positive thickness–Tc trend. Uncontrolled confounds—substrate identity, post-deposition annealing, and deposition process variation across labs—prevent any causal attribution of Tc differences to thickness in this dataset.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: The 5 nm Wu_2026 silicon sample (Tc = 10.0 K) is the thinnest film and shares the lowest Tc, which is weakly consistent with a thinner-film → suppressed-Tc narrative; however, the effect magnitude is only ~2 K and is not isolable from substrate and process differences.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: The 50 nm Wu_2026 AlN-sapphire sample—the thickest in the set—also shows Tc = 10.0 K, identical to the thinnest film and directly contradicting a positive thickness–Tc relationship; this sample also received a 1000°C anneal, making thickness and thermal processing effects entirely entangled.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: A controlled thickness series (e.g., 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 nm) deposited in a single ALD run on the same substrate without post-deposition annealing, with co-located XRD/TEM stoichiometry characterization and resistivity–temperature curves, is the minimum dataset needed to isolate a thickness effect on Tc.

writeup.finding_detail: The dataset contains four NbN samples: two Böttcher_2025 samples (both 20 nm, both Tc = 12.0 K, ALE-thinned from bulk, no anneal), Wu_2026 on silicon (5 nm, Tc = 10.0 K, no anneal), and Wu_2026 on AlN-sapphire (50 nm, Tc = 10.0 K, 1000°C anneal). The two Böttcher_2025 samples are effectively replicates—they contribute no independent thickness variation and cannot inform a thickness–Tc trend. The Wu_2026 pair spans the widest thickness range (5 vs. 50 nm) but yields identical Tc values, falsifying a simple monotonic relationship within that pair alone. The seed correlation cited (Chang_2025, n=1) concerns Au-encapsulation proximity-effect suppression of the superconducting gap—a mechanistically distinct phenomenon from intrinsic NbN film thickness effects on Tc—and should not be interpreted as direct evidence for this hypothesis. The Tc spread observed (10–12 K) is plausibly explained entirely by substrate-dependent strain and epitaxial quality, nitrogen stoichiometry variation between deposition runs, and the confounding 1000°C anneal on the AlN-sapphire sample.
writeup.alternative_explanations: Substrate identity (silicon vs. sapphire vs. AlN-on-sapphire) and post-deposition annealing are the most plausible primary drivers of the observed Tc variation, acting through lattice mismatch, strain, and stoichiometric restructuring; nitrogen stoichiometry (N/Nb ratio), which is sensitive to ALD precursor chemistry and plasma conditions rather than thickness per se, is an additional dominant confound that could account for the full 2 K Tc spread independently of film thickness.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is very low (0.15) for four compounding reasons: (1) only 4 samples are available, two of which are replicates providing no thickness variation; (2) the one pair spanning the widest thickness range (5 nm vs. 50 nm) shows identical Tc, actively contradicting the hypothesis; (3) substrate, annealing, and deposition process are not controlled across any comparison pair, making causal attribution impossible; and (4) the single author-stated seed correlation involves a different physical mechanism in a different experimental context. The dataset is insufficient in size, range, and experimental control to support or refute the hypothesis.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM materials-to-device mapping function of the form Tc = f(film_thickness) for ALD-NbN is not supportable from this dataset. The data do not permit even a qualitative directional mapping (e.g., monotonically increasing or decreasing), let alone a quantitative functional form. For QREM purposes, film thickness should currently be treated as a weakly constrained secondary parameter for NbN, with substrate identity and deposition process (captured as categorical or lab-specific variables) likely carrying more predictive weight for Tc. A mapping function could only be proposed after a controlled thickness series from a single deposition run is available; at that point, a piecewise or saturating function (reflecting Anderson localization at ultra-thin limits and bulk-like behavior above ~20 nm) would be the physically motivated candidate form to test.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: NbN is the only material class represented in this hypothesis corpus. With only 4 samples—2 of which are replicates—NbN does not yet have sufficient independent thickness variation to warrant a focused re-analysis of this specific hypothesis. A re-analysis is recommended only after the corpus is expanded to ≥8 NbN samples with explicit thickness variation from controlled deposition series. No other material classes are represented and none can be recommended for re-analysis under this hypothesis at this time.
questions_for_reviewer: The Wu_2026 AlN-sapphire sample received a 1000°C anneal while all other samples in this set did not—is this anneal temperature known to restructure NbN stoichiometry or grain boundaries sufficiently to suppress Tc by ~2 K independently of thickness, and should this sample be excluded from any thickness–Tc comparison on those grounds?

The Böttcher_2025 samples are described as ALE-thinned from a bulk film to 20 nm—does the ALE thinning process introduce surface damage or stoichiometric changes that make these samples non-representative of as-deposited 20 nm NbN films, and should their Tc be interpreted as a thinned-film value rather than a native thin-film value?

The seed correlation (Chang_2025, n=1) involves Au encapsulation suppressing the superconducting gap via proximity effect—is there any published evidence that this mechanism produces a measurable shift in the resistive Tc (as opposed to the gap energy), and if not, should this seed be retired as a basis for the thickness–Tc hypothesis?

Are there additional NbN thickness series in the literature (e.g., Shearrow et al. 2018, Hazra et al. 2016) that could be ingested into the corpus to provide the controlled thickness variation currently absent, and would those datasets use comparable ALD deposition methods or primarily sputtered films requiring a separate hypothesis branch?
writeup.suggested_followup: The highest-value next step is ingestion of published controlled NbN thickness series into the corpus—specifically studies that vary thickness systematically on a single substrate type from a single deposition run (e.g., sputtered or ALD NbN on silicon or sapphire at 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 nm) with reported Tc, sheet resistance, and ideally XRD data. If no such dataset exists in the literature for ALD-NbN specifically, a targeted deposition experiment depositing a thickness series in a single ALD campaign on silicon substrates without post-annealing, with four-probe Tc and XRD characterization, would directly resolve the hypothesis with approximately 6–8 samples.

writeup.title: Nominal inverse trend between Tc and microwave loss tangent in tantalum films is fully confounded by crystal phase, substrate, and transport regime across only 4 samples
writeup.hypothesis: Higher superconducting critical temperature (Tc_K) is associated with lower microwave loss tangent (derived_tan_delta) in tantalum-based resonator films, such that improving Tc does not penalize and may even improve microwave coherence.
writeup.summary: Across 4 tantalum resonator samples, a directionally consistent inverse trend between Tc and derived_tan_delta is nominally visible — alpha-Ta samples (Tc = 4.2 K) show lower loss than a beta-Ta sample (Tc = 0.7 K) — but this pattern is entirely explainable by crystal phase, substrate choice, and transport regime differences rather than Tc itself. Within the three Bland_2025 samples sharing identical Tc = 4.2 K, derived_tan_delta varies by ~19%, demonstrating that Tc is not a strong predictor of loss even within a single material family. The data are insufficient to support or refute the hypothesis, and the result is genuinely inconclusive rather than a confirmed null finding.

evidence_summary.key_supporting: The single contrasting data point — Joshi_2026 beta-Ta on sapphire (Tc = 0.7 K, derived_tan_delta = 0.0016) versus Bland_2025 alpha-Ta on Si (Tc = 4.2 K, derived_tan_delta = 0.00095–0.00113) — is directionally consistent with the hypothesis that higher Tc accompanies lower microwave loss.
evidence_summary.key_complicating: The two sample groups differ simultaneously in at least three major confounding variables (Ta crystal phase, substrate material, and transport regime), making it impossible to attribute any tan_delta difference to Tc; furthermore, within-group variation at fixed Tc = 4.2 K (~19% spread across Bland_2025 samples) indicates that Tc alone does not determine tan_delta.
evidence_summary.missing_evidence: Alpha-Ta and beta-Ta films on identical substrates with matched deposition conditions and intermediate Tc values (1.5–3.5 K, achievable via controlled alloying or annealing) are needed to decouple crystal phase from Tc as the causal variable; Ta-Hf alloy resonator data (as in the seeding Yang_2026 claim) with both Tc_K and derived_tan_delta measured are also absent from the corpus.

writeup.finding_detail: The dataset comprises three Bland_2025 alpha-Ta-on-Si samples (Tc = 4.2 K; derived_tan_delta = 0.00095–0.00113 across aged, CPW, and lumped-element geometries) and one Joshi_2026 beta-Ta-on-sapphire sample (Tc = 0.7 K; derived_tan_delta = 0.0016). The apparent inverse Tc–tan_delta relationship rests entirely on the contrast between these two groups, which differ in crystal phase (alpha vs. beta), substrate (Si vs. Al2O3), and transport regime (clean vs. dirty limit) — any one of which is independently sufficient to explain the observed tan_delta difference. Alpha-Ta is well established in the literature to have lower intrinsic microwave loss than beta-Ta, making crystal phase the most parsimonious explanation for the observed pattern without invoking Tc as a causal variable. The seeding literature claim (Yang_2026, Ta-Hf alloy) is not represented in the measured corpus, so the specific scenario of Tc improvement without loss penalty in an alloy context cannot be evaluated. The derived_tan_delta field carries additional cross-sample uncertainty because it aggregates multiple loss channels (TLS, quasiparticle, radiative, conductor) and is sensitive to resonator geometry and measurement power, as evidenced by the non-trivial spread among Bland_2025 samples at fixed Tc.
writeup.alternative_explanations: The most plausible alternative is that Ta crystal phase (alpha vs. beta) is the true underlying variable driving both Tc and tan_delta simultaneously, with substrate-dependent TLS loss and transport-regime-dependent quasiparticle loss as additional independent contributors — none of which require a direct causal link between Tc and microwave loss tangent.
writeup.confidence_rationale: Confidence is 0.2 (low) due to: only 4 total samples providing negligible statistical power; three of four samples from a single study (Bland_2025) with identical Tc, meaning the entire apparent trend rests on one contrasting data point (Joshi_2026); that single contrasting sample differs in at least three major confounding variables simultaneously; the seeding literature claim (Yang_2026) is unrepresented in the measured corpus; and within-group tan_delta variation at fixed Tc among Bland_2025 samples (~19% spread) demonstrates Tc is not a strong predictor of tan_delta. The directional consistency with the hypothesis prevents a confidence rating below 0.15, but the severe confounding and minimal sample size preclude any rating above 0.25.
writeup.qrem_implications: A QREM materials-to-device mapping function of the form tan_delta = f(Tc) is not supportable from this corpus. The data do not provide evidence that Tc can serve as a reliable predictor input for microwave loss in a mapping layer: within-material variation at fixed Tc is comparable in magnitude to the cross-material variation attributed to Tc differences, and the dominant confounders (crystal phase, substrate, transport regime) are not controlled. If a mapping function were to be constructed, it would need to encode crystal phase and substrate identity as primary categorical inputs, with Tc as a secondary continuous variable whose independent contribution remains unquantified. Until controlled experiments isolating Tc as an independent variable are available, any QREM mapping that uses Tc as a proxy for tan_delta in Ta films risks conflating phase-selection effects with a spurious Tc dependence.
writeup.per_material_recommendation: No material class in the current corpus has sufficient samples to warrant a statistically meaningful focused re-analysis of this specific hypothesis. Ta has 4 samples but they are too heavily confounded for isolation of the Tc effect. A focused re-analysis would become warranted for Ta if at least 8–10 samples spanning a continuous range of Tc values (achievable via alloying or annealing) on a single substrate type with matched geometry were added. Nb and NbTiN are absent from this corpus entirely and should be considered for a parallel hypothesis test if those corpora are larger, as they would provide a cross-material check on whether any Tc–tan_delta relationship is Ta-specific or universal.
questions_for_reviewer: Is the Yang_2026 Ta-Hf alloy claim (the seeding correlation) based on samples where both Tc_K and a resonator-derived loss tangent were measured on the same film, or were these properties measured on separate samples or inferred indirectly? This determines whether the seeding claim itself is directly testable from existing data.

For the Joshi_2026 beta-Ta-on-sapphire sample, is the reported Tc = 0.7 K a bulk film value or a suppressed value due to film thickness or disorder? If it reflects disorder-driven Tc suppression rather than an intrinsic beta-Ta property, the transport regime confound is even more severe than assessed here.

Do the Bland_2025 aged, CPW, and lumped-element samples represent the same physical film measured in different configurations, or independently deposited films? If the same film, the ~19% tan_delta spread is attributable to geometry and measurement protocol rather than material variation, which would further reduce the effective sample count for material-level inference.

Is there any existing Ta or Ta-alloy dataset in the broader literature where Tc was systematically varied (e.g., via nitrogen doping, Hf alloying, or annealing) while holding substrate and crystal phase constant, that could be added to the corpus to directly test this hypothesis?
writeup.suggested_followup: The most valuable next experiment is deposition of a series of Ta films on identical substrates (e.g., high-resistivity Si) spanning a continuous Tc range from ~0.7 K to ~4.4 K, achieved by controlled Hf or N alloying or post-deposition annealing, with crystal phase verified by XRD for each sample, followed by resonator fabrication and loss tangent measurement at matched geometry and power — this would for the first time allow Tc to be varied as a near-independent variable while holding phase, substrate, and geometry approximately constant.
